perturbation.original_text: The Adviser shall at all times conform to: (i) all applicable provisions of the Act and any rules and regulations adopted thereunder; (ii) the provisions of the Registration Statement of the Trust under the Securities Act of 1933 and the Act as amended from time to time; (iii) the provisions of the Agreement and Declaration of Trust and the By-Laws of the Trust; and (iv) any other applicable provisions of state and federal law.
perturbation.changed_text: The Adviser shall endeavor to conform to: (i) all applicable provisions of the Act and any rules and regulations adopted thereunder; (ii) the provisions of the Registration Statement of the Trust under the Securities Act of 1933 and the Act as amended from time to time; (iii) the provisions of the Agreement and Declaration of Trust and the By-Laws of the Trust; and (iv) applicable provisions of state and federal law.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall at all times conform' to 'shall endeavor to conform' introduces ambiguity and weakens the obligation to comply with all laws. Removing 'any other' before 'applicable provisions of state and federal law' adds vagueness.
perturbation.law_citation: Section 34(b) of the Investment Company Act of 1940
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 34(b) of the Investment Company Act of 1940 makes it unlawful for any person to make untrue statements or omit facts in registration statements and other documents filed with the SEC. The original text ensures the Adviser conforms to all applicable laws, offering some assurance against such violations. By changing 'shall at all times conform' to 'shall endeavor to conform', the modified text implies a less stringent obligation, potentially increasing the risk of non-compliance with the law, because the adviser does not have a legal obligation to conform at all times.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: This page is better viewed with a JavaScript enabled browser. Document not Found Document not found The document you were looking for does not exist. Click here to close this window, or click here to go back.
perturbation.justification: The original text states "The Adviser shall at all times conform," creating a strict obligation. The changed text weakens this to "The Adviser shall endeavor to conform," which suggests an attempt rather than a guarantee of compliance. The Law Explanation states that Section 34(b) of the Investment Company Act of 1940 makes it unlawful for any person to make untrue statements or omit facts in registration statements and other documents filed with the SEC, and that the original text offers some assurance against such violations. Changing 'shall at all times conform' to 'shall endeavor to conform' implies a less stringent obligation, potentially increasing the risk of non-compliance with the law. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is a 'Document not Found' error and Scraped Law Snippet 2 is empty, the Law Explanation is strong enough to create a legal contradiction, as it means the adviser does not have a legal obligation to conform at all times, potentially creating an Ambiguous Legal Obligation.

perturbation.original_text: The Adviser shall create and maintain all necessary records in accordance with all applicable laws, rules and regulations, including, but not limited to, records required by Section 31(a) of the Act and the rules thereunder, as the same may be amended from time to time, pertaining to the various functions performed by it and not otherwise created and maintained by another party pursuant to contract with the Trust.
perturbation.changed_text: The Adviser shall make efforts to create and maintain records as it deems reasonably necessary, considering applicable laws, rules and regulations, including records related to Section 31(a) of the Act and rules thereunder, as amended, for functions performed by it and not maintained by another party through contract with the Trust.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'shall create and maintain all necessary records in accordance with all applicable laws' with 'shall make efforts to create and maintain records as it deems reasonably necessary, considering applicable laws' introduces subjectivity. This contradicts the explicit record-keeping requirements under Section 31(a) of the Investment Company Act of 1940.
perturbation.law_citation: Section 31(a) of the Investment Company Act of 1940 and Rule 31a-1
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 31(a) of the Investment Company Act of 1940 and Rule 31a-1 mandates specific record-keeping requirements for investment companies and their advisers. By allowing the Adviser to determine what is 'reasonably necessary', the modified text allows for potentially inadequate record-keeping, in direct violation of the law. The original text has the adviser fully responsible for creating and maintaining records in accordance with all applicable laws, but the updated text only has them 'making efforts.'

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 CFR § 270.31a-1 - Records to be maintained by registered investment companies, certain majority-owned subsidiaries thereof, and other persons having transactions with registered investment companies. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 270.31a-1 - Records to be maintained by registered investment companies, certain majority-owned subsidiaries thereof, and other persons having transactions with registered investment companies. CFR Table of Popular Names prev | next § 270.31a-1 Records to be maintained by registered investment companies, certain majority-owned subsidiaries thereof, and other persons having transactions with registered investment companies. (a) Every registered investment company, and every underwriter , broker, dealer, or investment adviser which is a majority-owned subsidiary of such a company, shall maintain and keep current the accounts, books, and other documents relating to its business which constitute the record forming the basis for financial statements required to be filed pursuant to section 30 of the Investment Company Act of 1940 and of the auditor's certificates relating thereto. (b) Every registered investment company shall maintain and keep current the following books, accounts, and other documents: (1) Journals (or other records of original entry) containing an itemized daily record in detail of all purchases and sales of securities (including sales and redemptions of its own securities), all receipts and deliveries of securities (including certificate numbers if such detail is not recorded by custodian or transfer agent), all receipts and disbursements of cash and all other debits and credits. Such records shall show for each such transaction the name and quantity of securities, the unit and aggregate purchase or sale price, commission paid, the market on which effected, the trade date, the settlement date, and the name of the person through or from whom purchased or received or to whom sold or delivered. In the case of a money market fund , also identify the provider of any Demand Feature or Guarantee (as defined in § 270.2a-7(a)(9) or § 270.2a-7(a)(16) respectively) and give a brief description of the nature of the Demand Feature or Guarantee (e.g., unconditional demand feature , conditional demand feature , letter of credit, or bond insurance) and, in a subsidiary portfolio investment record, provide the complete legal name and accounting and other information (including sufficient information to calculate coupons, accruals, maturities, puts, and calls) necessary to identify, value, and account for each investment. (2) General and auxiliary ledgers (or other records) reflecting all assets , liability, reserve, capital, income and expense accounts, including: (i) Separate ledger accounts (or other records) reflecting the following: (a) Securities in transfer; (b) Securities in physical possession; (c) Securities borrowed and securities loaned; (d) Monies borrowed and monies loaned (together with a record of the collateral therefor and substitutions in such collateral); (e) Dividends
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity and weakens the original obligation to maintain necessary records as required by law. By changing 'shall create and maintain all necessary records in accordance with all applicable laws' to 'shall make efforts to create and maintain records as it deems reasonably necessary, considering applicable laws,' the modified contract allows the Adviser to subjectively determine what records are necessary, potentially leading to non-compliance with Section 31(a) of the Investment Company Act of 1940. Scraped Law Snippet 2, referencing 17 CFR § 270.31a-1, states that investment advisers 'shall maintain and keep current the accounts, books, and other documents relating to its business'. The change from 'shall' to 'make efforts' and the addition of 'as it deems reasonably necessary' directly contradicts the mandatory nature of record-keeping as described in the law snippet. This introduces a clear violation by weakening the obligation.

perturbation.original_text: The Adviser agrees to defend and indemnify the Trust, and its trustees, officers and agent from and against any and all liability, losses, claims and damages arising from any inaccurate or incomplete statement provided by the Adviser (or its officers, employees, directors and agents) for inclusion in the Fund's prospectus or statement of additional information.
perturbation.changed_text: The Adviser agrees to consider defending and indemnifying the Trust, and its trustees, officers and agent from liability, losses, claims and damages potentially arising from a significantly inaccurate statement knowingly provided by the Adviser for inclusion in the Fund's prospectus or statement of additional information.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text weakens the indemnification obligation. 'Agrees to consider' makes the action discretionary. Adding 'potentially arising from a significantly inaccurate statement knowingly provided' raises the bar for triggering indemnification, contradicting SEC regulations that require accurate prospectuses.
perturbation.law_citation: Section 11 of the Securities Act of 1933
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 11 of the Securities Act of 1933 imposes liability for untrue statements or omissions in a registration statement (including a prospectus). By limiting indemnification to 'significantly inaccurate statement knowingly provided', the modified text undermines the Trust's protection against liabilities arising from less severe, but still actionable, inaccuracies, or inaccuracies due to negligence rather than deliberate intent. The word consideration implies that they may not provide these protections.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: This page is better viewed with a JavaScript enabled browser. Document not Found Document not found The document you were looking for does not exist. Click here to close this window, or click here to go back.
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity and weakens the indemnification obligation. The original text states the Adviser 'agrees to defend and indemnify,' whereas the changed text states the Adviser 'agrees to consider defending and indemnifying.' This introduces discretion where there was none before. The addition of 'potentially arising from a significantly inaccurate statement knowingly provided' also raises the bar for indemnification, requiring a higher level of intent and severity for the obligation to trigger. According to the Law Explanation, Section 11 of the Securities Act of 1933 imposes liability for untrue statements or omissions. The change to 'significantly inaccurate statement knowingly provided' undermines the Trust's protection against liabilities arising from less severe inaccuracies or inaccuracies due to negligence. Although both snippets are unavailable, the Law and Perturbation Explanations sufficiently demonstrate the legal contradiction created by the ambiguous obligation.
